title: Balancing AI slop
author: Sulakshana
text:
Balancing AI slop
We are living in the era of AI slop. Harari lays this out in Sapiens (and revisits it in Nexus): Objective facts exist independently of us, subjective facts live only in one person’s mind and intersubjective facts exist in the shared stories that many people believe and act on. Take a rock or an apple. It exists whether or not we talk about it. In our everyday view, it is made of atoms and molecules, and when light hits it, certain wavelengths reflect into our eyes. It objectively exists. This is objective information.
Nations, religion, banks, and currency exist because and only because people talk about them and act as if they are real. They live in the information passed from person to person. They are intersubjective. The power of the intersubjective is that it becomes real in practice because we coordinate around it. The more we talk about them, the more they take shape and become real in practice.
Because humans believe in things that do not exist independently of our minds, we spread those beliefs online too. The internet holds well-researched work, but it also carries interpretations that are watered down to be more palatable. When you ask a large language model like ChatGPT for information, it converts your words into tokens and predicts the next likely token, assembling sentences from statistical patterns. That process can sometimes produce confident statements that go beyond the available evidence, although we have modes to fact check/think/prompt better. Creators have started warning about this dynamic. In the scenario of Kurzgesagt (one of my most loved channels for quality information video content), they had attempted to use AI for research, while also getting it fact checked by real human researchers. They had come to find that AI had added some of its own information, to seem to agree with you, and extrapolated the existing information to an extent. It has also used an AI article without sources, used as a credible source for AI research. This is where the death of the internet begins. Now there is a proper source of misinformation online. When the next AI repeats the same research, it will find a transcript from a video with a lot of views. The misinformation is now true. It will spread.
Now what happens when we generate content with AI? As advertises that it does it quick, fast and in an efficient manner? In this era of quick content generation and emphasis on productiveness, content creators want to create and push out content as quickly as possible. Employees are pushed to be more productive and use AI for research, sometimes not fact checking their information. This is where the incentives of the modern web matter. Fact-checking turns into a nice-to-have. Meanwhile, bots bend what is rewarded and surfaced. In recent measurements, roughly half of all internet traffic is non-human, and about one third is “bad bot” traffic. That alone shifts what gets visibility. On Youtube, new channels publish long form videos multiple times a week with AI generated thumbnails, voices and scripts. True crime, video essays, science — no space is safe. We’re in the golden era of soulless slop.
Platforms amplify the slop. Search products now summarize pages in the results themselves, which multiple studies and publisher groups say reduces clicks to original sources. Less traffic means fewer resources for reporting and research, and a stronger pull toward fast summaries.
Music is seeing its own wave. Streaming platforms have announced large-scale removals of spammy or suspected AI-generated tracks and are rolling out “responsible AI” policies with major labels. When the friction to produce drops to near zero, the feed starts to fill with zero-value content.
So we end up in a world where objective, subjective, and intersubjective realities all compete on the same screen. We spend more time online, and we try to build a sanctuary away from potholes, noise, and pollution. We curate information to make the world we want, but the same mechanics also make it easy to curate misinformation into something that feels real.
This is not a pessimistic view on AI. It is a call to be deliberate. Original thought, primary research, and firsthand work are how we rise above the slop. The question, then, is how do we actually do it?
- Choose sources, not summaries. When a search result or feed gives you an AI overview, click through and read the source. It is slower and better.
- Follow people who do primary work. Reward those who publish methods, data, and citations. That is how intersubjective truths stay tethered to the world.
- Keep a small personal canon. For any topic you care about, hold a short list of originals you trust. Revisit them before you post or decide.
- Write from what you have touched. Add your own observations, interviews, screenshots, and experiments.
- Show your scaffolding. Link to sources, note limits, and be explicit about what you are unsure of. Trust grows when readers can check your work.
- Use AI as a lens, not a voice. Let it help you find counter-arguments and references, then verify each claim and rewrite in your words.
- Practice small rituals of verification. Check dates, look for the first source, and be wary of confident language with no citations.
AI will keep getting faster. That does not mean we have to. The point is not to outproduce the slop. The point is to raise our bar for what we consume and what we make.
Truth still starts in the world and in the work. Touch something. Read the source. Talk to a person. Run a small experiment. Then write from that. The internet needs more first hand notes and fewer copies of copies.
